Morgan"},"description":"Hi! The Ripped Body® Method is an evidence-based, data-driven approach to physique transformation. ⠀ For the majority of my 20s, I fell for the scams of the fitness industry. 🤦🏻‍♂️ ⠀ This site is my revenge. 😬 ⠀ Since 2011, I’ve written guides explaining how we get results to help readers avoid the same path of frustration. We’ve helped over 2,000 busy working guys transform their bodies WITHOUT supplements, fad diets, or the process dominating their lives. ⠀ 📩 Enter your email address in the box at the bottom of this page and I'd be honored to show you how.","sameAs":["https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/","https:\/\/www.facebook.com\/rippedbodycom","https:\/\/www.instagram.com\/andy_rippedbody\/","https:\/\/andymorgan.com\/","https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Andy-John-Morgan\/e\/B07Q2J5ST6\/ref=ntt_dp_epwbk_2","https:\/\/x.com\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/RippedBodyjp","https:\/\/muscleandstrengthpyramids.com\/","https:\/\/soundcloud.com\/andyrippedbodyjp","morganandrew"]},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696633755","position":1,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696633755","name":"What should I eat to bulk?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"The foods you should eat to bulk are the same as those when you cut; you'll simply have more of them and room for 'treats.'<br\/><br\/>Your diet should be rich in fruits, vegetables, and have sufficient protein (<a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/macros\/\">1 g\/lb of body weight per day<\/a>). The rest should be made up of predominantly fresh and unprocessed foods. These things will help keep you full when dieting, help ensure muscle mass retention, and cover your micronutritional needs.<br\/><br\/>This advice does not change when you bulk. However, because you have a higher calorie budget, you can fit in calorie-dense treats that you might crave from time to time. These should not come at the cost of nutrient-dense foods (fruits and vegetables).<br\/><br\/>Most clients eat the same kind of diet year-round; they just increase or decrease their carb intake and frequency that they eat out. I like to have a bowl of cereal with a chocolate protein shake made with skimmed milk to meet my carb requirements. Here's my <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-count-macros\/\">guide to making meals out of your macros<\/a>.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696659281","position":2,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696659281","name":"How can I bulk up fast?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Most answers to the question of how to bulk up fast focus on foods people should eat. But the simple fact of the matter is that there is no way to bulk up faster than your genetic potential. If you do, you're just getting fat.<br\/><br\/>Recall from the start of this guide that how fast you can grow depends on your genetics, calorie intake, training quality, sufficient protein intake, sleep quality, and stress levels. Make sure you take each of these seriously. Refer to the <em>How Quickly Can We Gain Muscle As We Bulk?<\/em> section, so that you know what rate of weight gain to target. This will stop you from progressing more slowly than you otherwise could, but more commonly, getting fat.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696671178","position":3,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696671178","name":"Are bananas good for bulking?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"The average 125 g banana has 27 g of carbs with just 3 g of fiber. This means they're relatively high in carbs and low in fiber, so you're unlikely to poop yourself if you eat a lot of bananas to hit your carb requirements (unlike with other fruits).<br\/><br\/>However, roughly half of the carbs will be fructose content. Fructose is processed differently to the glucose you get from the carbs in rice, potatoes, bread, and pasta. So while it would be fine to eat several bananas per day, it's probably not optimal to rely on them exclusively to meet your carbohydrate needs.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696686853","position":4,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696686853","name":"Is rice good for bulking up?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Yes, rice is good for bulking up but no better than pasta, bread, potatoes, or other starchy sources to meet your carb requirements. Feel free to choose what you enjoy and make sure you don't skimp on protein intake, of the other 9 critical factors for bulking up that I cover in this guide.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696710321","position":5,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696710321","name":"Where can I find out about macros, meal timing, and supplements?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"These are covered in my <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/complete-diet-nutrition-set-up-guide\/\">Nutrition Setup Guide<\/a>.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696734495","position":6,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696734495","name":"How should I train?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Click the training tab in the menu to see a list of sample programs from my <a rel=\"noreferrer noopener\" href=\"https:\/\/muscleandstrengthpyramids.com\/\" target=\"_blank\">Muscle and Strength Training Pyramid book<\/a>.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696759116","position":7,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/#faq-question-1596696759116","name":"What should I do when I want to cut?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Here's my <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-adjust-macros\/\">guide for macro adjustments when cutting<\/a>. But if this topic interests you, check out my book: <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/adjustments-manual\/\"><strong>The Diet Adjustments Manual<\/strong><\/a>.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"}]}</script> <link href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin="anonymous" rel="preconnect"><link rel="alternate" title="oEmbed (JSON)" type="application/json+oembed" href="https://rippedbody.com/wp-json/oembed/1.0/embed?url=https%3A%2F%2Frippedbody.com%2Fhow-to-bulk%2F"><link rel="alternate" title="oEmbed (XML)" type="text/xml+oembed" href="https://rippedbody.com/wp-json/oembed/1.0/embed?url=https%3A%2F%2Frippedbody.com%2Fhow-to-bulk%2F&format=xml"><style id="wp-img-auto-sizes-contain-inline-css">img:is([sizes=auto i],[sizes^="auto," i]){contain-intrinsic-size:3000px 1500px}

  // Helper function to read a cookie value
  function getCookie(name) {
    var value = "; " + document.cookie;
    var parts = value.split("; " + name + "=");
    if (parts.length == 2) return parts.pop().split(";").shift();
  }

  // Helper function to set a cookie
  function setCookie(name, value, days) {
    var expires = "";
    if (days) {
      var date = new Date();
      date.setTime(date.getTime() + (days*24*60*60*1000));
      expires = "; expires=" + date.toUTCString();
    }
    document.cookie = name + "=" + (value || "")  + expires + "; path=/";
  }

  // Function to update display eligibility based on the time gap
  function updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds) {
    setTimeout(function() {
      window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
      console.log('updateDisplayEligibility - OptinMonster is now display eligible');
    }, waitTimeSeconds * 1000); // Convert seconds to milliseconds
  }

  document.addEventListener('om.Campaign.show', function(event) {
    // console.log('om.Campaign.show', event.detail.Campaign);

    var now = new Date().getTime();
    setCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp', now, 1); // Store timestamp in cookie for 1 day
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';

    updateDisplayEligibility(minimumDisplayGap);
  });

  var now = new Date().getTime();
  var lastDisplayTime = parseInt(getCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp')) || 0;
  var timePassed = (now - lastDisplayTime) / 1000; // Convert milliseconds to seconds

  if (timePassed >= minimumDisplayGap) {
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
    console.log('OptinMonster display eligible on page load');
  } else {
    var waitTimeSeconds = minimumDisplayGap - timePassed;
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';
    updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds);
  }</script> <style id="uagb-style-conditional-extension">@media (min-width: 1025px){body .uag-hide-desktop.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-desktop{display:none !important}}@media (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1024px){body .uag-hide-tab.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-tab{display:none !important}}@media (max-width: 767px){body .uag-hide-mob.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-mob{display:none !important}}</style><style id="uagb-style-frontend-62682">.uagb-heading-text mark {

#555555 !important;}.formkit-form[data-uid="8462769b72"] .formkit-submit{border:1px solid #333333 !important;}</style></form><div id="modal-ready"><p>To bulk up means to gain bodyweight with the primary goal of gaining muscle.</p><p>There is a lack of quality diet advice out there for people trying to bulk. ‘Eat more!’ lacks the detail and finesse to optimize things, ‘Calculate your protein, carb, and fat macros, then just train hard!’ misses a fundamental point — as we advance, calorie and macronutrient needs change, and adjustments are necessary to keep us progressing.</p><p>For this reason, we can’t just use a <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/">macro calculator</a> and expect to get jacked and juicy without adjusting anything thereafter.</p><p>Sure, it’s a good place to start but you’ll get stuck pretty quickly. Perhaps you’ve experienced this already and that’s why you are here?</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em>How much more should we eat? Of what macro?</em><br><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em>Do macros matter?</em><br><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em>How do I minimize fat gain?</em><br><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em>How do I know when I should adjust my food intake?</em></p><p>You have these questions and many more. This guide has your answers. It’s something people had been bugging me to write for years, but I didn’t feel that I had enough experience to do so without merely parroting things I had read elsewhere and applied to only a handful of clients.</p><p>I’m glad that I waited, as <a href="https://rippedbody.com/coaching/" data-wpil-monitor-id="44">coaching</a> a lot of people how to bulk up is the only way I could find out what stuff matters vs. what doesn’t, and come up with my own way of doing things.</p><p>This is detailed because it needs to be if you’re going to do this right. But it will save you months of wasted effort down the line. I hope you find it interesting as well as practically helpful when choosing how you wish to bulk up and implementing it successfully.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><p><strong>CONTENTS</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><a href="#factors"><strong>Part 1</strong>: Factors That Determine The Rate We Can Gain Muscle As We Bulk Up</a></li><li><a href="#how-quickly"><strong>Part 2:</strong> How Quickly We Can Expect To Gain Muscle As We Bulk Up</a></li><li><a href="#ways"><strong>Part 3:</strong> The Three Ways To Bulk Up</a></li><li><a href="#comparisons"><strong>Part 4:</strong> How to Bulk Up — Comparing Fat and Muscle Gain With Different Methods</a></li></ul><p><strong>Who this guide is for:</strong></p><p>This is a guide to adjusting for a bulk. I have a separate <a href="https://rippedbody.com/free-nutrition-course/">Nutrition Setup Guide</a>, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/training/">Training Guide</a>, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/cut-or-bulk/">Bulking vs. Cutting guide</a>. This is for:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>Those that have finished their cut and are looking to turn it around into a bulk.</li><li>Those that are currently bulking but have stopped gaining weight and aren’t sure how to make further changes to keep progressing.</li><li>Those that have made an initial setup calculation for their bulk and are wondering what comes next.</li></ol><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="factors">PART 1: FACTORS THAT DETERMINE THE RATE WE CAN GAIN MUSCLE AS WE BULK UP</h2><p><em>If you understand the important factors for muscle growth, you’ll be able to troubleshoot if you run into any issues. So, pay special attention to this section, because you may find the solution to your problems right here.</em></p><p>There is a limit to the amount of muscle mass that we can grow. This is different for everyone.</p><p>What is true to all is that the first year of <em>serious</em> training will earn you the biggest gains, with diminishing returns after that. This means that you can look great after just a few years of training, and those years will give you the greatest rewards, as long as you do things right.</p><p>Hypothetically, the path to your maximum muscular potential looks as follows.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img decoding="async" width="1024" height="996" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2-1024x996.png" alt="The Path To Maximum Muscular Potential v2" class="wp-image-59545" style="width:512px;height:498px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2-1024x996.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2-450x438.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2-768x747.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2-1536x1495.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2-1568x1526.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2.png 1596w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-v2-450x438@2x.png 900w" sizes="(max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 1. The possible path to your maximum muscular potential. You will make the most gains in your first years of serious training with diminishing returns after that.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>The dot represents the point where the maximum muscular potential is reached. The time frame is in years. The horizontal dotted line afterward shows that no further gains will be made, despite your training efforts, once you reach this point.</p><p>Practically, you will never know when you have quite reached your limit. And by the time you do (if you ever do), training will be a part of your life so you wouldn’t quit anyway and it’s not something worth worrying about.</p><p><strong>But most people stagnate way before they get remotely close to their genetic potential.</strong></p><p>Unless you have been training intelligently for over a decade, it’s best to assume that your stagnation is because you’re screwing something up.</p><p>I’m willing to bet that your journey up until now has not been linear. Some time ago, you stagnated for a while, had an “ahah!” moment, fixed the issue, then continued to progress, and then that progress has now slowed. Here’s how that looks:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="1021" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1024x1021.png" alt="The Realistic Path To Maximum Muscular Potential" class="wp-image-59546" style="width:512px;height:511px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1024x1021.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-450x450.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-150x150.png 150w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-768x766.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1536x1531.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1568x1563.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential.png 1583w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-450x450@2x.png 900w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Realistic-Path-To-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-150x150@2x.png 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 2. The realistic path to maximum muscular potential.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p class="has-text-align-left">The cause of my first training plateau was not eating enough. I was naturally very skinny (150 lbs / 68 kg at ~6’2). When I started training, I grew for a while (to 155 lbs / 72 kg), but then started to stagnate (point 1) and plateaued pretty quickly after that (point 2) at 162 lbs / 75 kg.</p><p><strong>It took me <em>five years</em> to realize this mistake — how to bulk up — then fix it</strong> (point 3)<strong>.</strong></p><p>My next plateau was caused by not training hard and frequently enough (point 4). But by the time I figured that out, I had bought every <a href="https://rippedbody.com/supplements/" data-wpil-monitor-id="107">supplement</a> under the sun, as the muscle mags told me, thinking that the next one was the cure. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🤦🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f926-1f3fb.svg"></p><p>How many of your fellow gym members look the same as they did two years ago? And yet, what percentage of them do you think have hit their genetic limit? And yet, how many of them take the time to look at the reasons why vs. just coming back day-after-day, repeating the same things, and expecting a different outcome?</p><p>The problem is that what brought them to where they are now will not take them to where they want to be. Something has to change, but they don’t know what. If this is you, this section of the guide will help you figure that out.</p><p>Here are the factors that determine the rate at which we can grow in descending order of importance. All but the first are under our control.</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Genetics (and drug use)</strong></li><li><strong>Calorie intake</strong></li><li><strong>Sufficient training stimulus to force adaptation</strong></li><li><strong>Sufficient protein intake</strong></li><li><strong>Sufficient sleep</strong></li><li><strong>Stress</strong></li><li><strong>Body-fat percentage</strong></li><li><strong>Everything else. </strong>(Fat-carb macronutrient intake, micronutrients, meal timing & frequency, supplements.)</li></ol><p>If you look at the list carefully, you’ll see that the top two items on the list — <em>genetics (and drug use)</em> and <em>calorie intake</em> — come ahead of training. What this means is that people can go about their training in a sub-optimal manner, but if they are genetically gifted enough and eat enough, they can get away with it.</p><p>This explains a lot of the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/charlatans-fitness-industry/">stupid advice out there in the  fitness industry</a>. These people do not need to pay as much attention to the rest of the factors. Most have decided not to.</p><h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="genetics">1. GENETICS (AND DRUG USE)</h3><p>Genetics play an enormous role in how well people respond to training.</p><p>We all know people at the gym who train with seemingly little effort but are huge. You probably know someone who grew like a weed when they started training and overtook you.</p><p>This is unfair, but so is life. Your genetics are genetics; you need to play the hand you are dealt with and be the best you can. Here’s how genetics affect the maximum rate of muscle growth.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="924" height="1024" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-924x1024.png" alt="The Effect of Genetic Difference on the Path to Maximum Muscular Potential" class="wp-image-59540" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-924x1024.png 924w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-406x450.png 406w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-693x768.png 693w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-1386x1536.png 1386w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-1568x1737.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1.png 1684w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Genetic-Difference-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-406x450@2x.png 812w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 924px) 100vw, 924px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 3. How <a href="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/" data-wpil-monitor-id="108">genetics affect the path to maximum muscular potential</a>. The “pre-training baseline” point will differ, and sporting background will determine how far along our lines we have already moved before we start lifting.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>The three lines show someone of <span style="color:#e9680c" class="tadv-color">average</span>, <span style="color:#098e5d" class="tadv-color">above-average</span>, and a <span style="color:#cf2e2e" class="tadv-color">below-average</span> genetic predisposition for muscle growth.</p><p>The main point I want to make here is that you shouldn’t look at someone who is bigger than you and assume that they have some special knowledge, and it’s prudent to not look at a smaller guy and assume they don’t know what they are doing.</p><p>Some of the most technically knowledgable people in the industry have good, but not outstanding physiques. This makes a lot of sense when you think about it — frustration with their own results led them to study harder.</p><p>Genetics for growth appear to be somewhat (not for all individuals, but when comparing populations) related to the starting point. We can see this when comparing growth rates for men and women. When they do the same program, absolute growth for men is higher, but they gain the same % of lean mass in relation to their starting lean mass. So, if you take two untrained individuals of the same height, with similar sporting histories, but different starting levels of lean mass, we can expect the bigger guy to grow bigger and more muscled overall. — Pretty much what common sense would tell us anyway.</p><p><strong><em>Should we worry about our genetics then?</em></strong></p><p>I’d argue not.</p><p>Whether you are genetically blessed, average, or screwed, the way you should approach things does not change.</p><p>It’s statistically unlikely that you’re in the genetically unlucky category and it doesn’t serve you well to <em>believe</em> that you are one of the unlucky ones either.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="606" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population-1024x606.png" alt="How Genetic Predisposition for Muscle Growth is Distributed Across The Population" class="wp-image-59543" style="width:768px;height:455px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population-1024x606.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population-450x266.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population-768x455.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population-1536x910.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population-1568x929.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population.png 1871w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-Genetic-Predisposition-for-Muscle-Growth-is-Distributed-Across-The-Population-450x266@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 4. The distribution of genetic luck for muscle growth. You may be one of the unlucky ones, but the odds are that you’re genetically average. So don’t throw yourself a pity party, assume that you can figure this out, and work with what you have got.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>The mind has an <em>incredibly</em> powerful effect on the body. So much so that you can actually give people sugar pills, tell them that they are steroids, and they will grow for a time like they are on steroids. (Yes, I shit you not that has <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="http://www.arielnet.com/articles/show/adi-pub-01242/anabolic-steroids-the-physiological-effects-of-placebos" target="_blank">actually been studied</a>.)</p><p>So while you probably know by now whether you’re one of the genetically lucky ones, it does not benefit you to buy into some sob story that you are one of the genetically unlucky ones either. The chances are that this isn’t the case anyway.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">How Drugs Affect Growth Rate and Muscular Potential</h4><p>Steroid use increases the <em>rate</em> at which you can grow, and allows for growth <em>beyond natural limits</em>.</p><p>There is a dose-response relationship, which means the more you use, the more pronounced this effect. For comparison, 5’10 bodybuilders competing in natural federations typically have a stage weight of 170–180 lbs (~78-82 kg). Seven-time Mr. Olympia winner, Phil Heath, is 5’9 (~175cm) and weighed an estimated 250 lbs (~114 kg) on stage.</p><p>So, at the extremes, drug use can make a <em>lot</em> of difference. This is represented by a dramatic shift upwards and inwards of the curve, orange to purple.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="913" height="1024" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-913x1024.png" alt="The Effect of Steroids on the Path to Maximum Muscular Potential" class="wp-image-59541" style="width:457px;height:512px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-913x1024.png 913w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-401x450.png 401w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-685x768.png 685w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-1370x1536.png 1370w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-1568x1758.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1.png 1634w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Effect-of-Steroids-on-the-Path-to-Maximum-Muscular-Potential-1-401x450@2x.png 802w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 913px) 100vw, 913px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 5. How drug use affects muscle growth. Note how both the rate of muscle growth and the total muscle growth achievable increases.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>Steroid use comes with considerable health risks, as well as potential jail time, depending on local laws. I do not recommend them, and I do not work with people who use them.</p><p>This is not a moral judgment, I just have neither the knowledge nor desire to go into this area. <strong>This guide is written for natural trainees who are looking to figure out what they need to do.</strong></p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">2. CALORIE INTAKE</h3><p>Failure to eat enough is top of the list for places where people go wrong when bulking. We cannot make something out of nothing.</p><p>Muscle growth is not optimized in a calorie deficit; however, at higher levels of body fat, our body can be in a calorie deficit and still build muscle. That is because your body will use fat stores to fuel itself.</p><p>However, as we get leaner and more advanced, this becomes increasingly difficult to achieve, and eventually, we will stop gaining any more muscle mass regardless of what we do.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="950" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake-1024x950.png" alt="Bulking Mistake - Insufficient Caloric Intake as You Bulk Up" class="wp-image-59529" style="width:512px;height:475px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake-1024x950.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake-450x417.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake-768x712.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake-1536x1424.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake-1568x1454.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake.png 1636w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Caloric-Intake-450x417@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 6. How insufficient calorie intake limits muscle growth over time. This is the cause of many people’s stagnation.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p><strong>If you are struggling to gain weight</strong> (your weight is not steadily increasing over the weeks), <em>you need to eat more</em>. It does not matter what your calculations said should happen: you may have miscalculated, you may be miscounting your food intake, or you might be a high <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-adjust-macros/" target="_blank">NEAT</a> responder. (I’ll come back to this later.)</p><p>• Related article: <em><a href="https://rippedbody.com/diet-tracking-vs-calculations/">Coaching Lessons #4 – Tracking Trumps Calculations</a></em></p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">3. APPROPRIATE TRAINING STIMULUS TO FORCE ADAPTATION</h3><p>It should go without saying that if you do not have sufficient training stimulus to force adaptations, then you will not grow muscle. The majority of the calorie surplus will be stored as fat. See my article, <em><a href="https://rippedbody.com/training-the-guide/">The Core Principles of Effective Training</a>,</em> for more on this.</p><h6 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">When there is an insufficient training stimulus, the weight you gain is almost exclusively fat</h6><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="703" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Training-Stimulus-Intake-1024x703.png" alt="Bulking Mistake - Insufficient Training Stimulus Intake as You Bulk Up" class="wp-image-59520" style="width:512px;height:352px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Training-Stimulus-Intake-1024x703.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Training-Stimulus-Intake-450x309.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Training-Stimulus-Intake-768x527.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Training-Stimulus-Intake.png 1207w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Training-Stimulus-Intake-450x309@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 7. Where there is an insufficient training stimulus, the weight gained when bulking is almost exclusively fat, rather than the 1:1 ratio of fat and muscle gain that is typical. This is also an issue many people face after they cut and realize they have achieved nothing.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">4. SUFFICIENT PROTEIN INTAKE</h3><p>You need to have a sufficient level of protein intake for tissue repair and growth. Insufficient protein intake will shift the curve in a similar way to insufficient calorie intake, but to a less severe degree.</p><p>How big the impact of is will depend on how far off of optimal intake is. I’ve tried to capture this here:</p><figure class="wp-block-image alignwide size-full"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="3073" height="1517" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake.png" alt="Bulking Mistake - Insufficient Protein Intake as You Bulk Up" class="wp-image-59530" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake.png 3073w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake-450x222.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake-1024x506.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake-768x379.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake-1536x758.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake-2048x1011.png 2048w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake-1568x774.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulking-Mistake-Insufficient-Protein-Intake-450x222@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 3073px) 100vw, 3073px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 8. Where there is insufficient protein intake (left), the curve shifts down to the right (right). The more protein is lacking, the greater the effect.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:40px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h3 class="wp-block-heading">5/6. SUFFICIENT SLEEP AND MANAGEMENT OF STRESS</h3><p>Insufficient sleep and a high level of stress will hamper recovery from your workouts and your growth. This becomes increasingly important as we progress. Before I take a coaching applicant on as a client, I check to see that these elements are on point first.</p><p>Please take your sleep and <a href="https://rippedbody.com/stress/" data-wpil-monitor-id="109">stress management</a> seriously, as it will flatten the curve and shift it down and towards the right, similar to what you see above.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> Related: <em><a href="https://rippedbody.com/training-life-stress-effect-program-progress/">Stress: In The Gym, Out of The Gym, and How it Affects Your Program and Progress</a></em></p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">7. BODY-FAT PERCENTAGE</h3><p>There are calorie partitioning benefits to being leaner.</p><p>This means that in general, the fatter we get, any calorie surplus has a tendency towards more fat storage than muscle growth. This happens because chronic, low-grade inflammation associated with obesity decreases the anabolic, and increases catabolic, signaling in the muscles.</p><p>You can still gain muscle (sumo wrestlers carry the highest muscle mass of any sport), it’s just that an increasing amount of the weight you gain will be fat.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="787" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks-1024x787.png" alt="How a Dream Bulk Looks" class="wp-image-59537" style="width:512px;height:394px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks-1024x787.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks-450x346.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks-768x590.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks-1536x1180.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks-1568x1205.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks.png 1851w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Dream-Bulk-looks-450x346@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 9. The fatter you get, the more fat you’ll store as you continue to bulk. Avoid <em><a href="https://rippedbody.com/dreambulkeritis/">dream bulking</a>, </em>where people simply eat their faces off, gaining an enormous amount of weight and kidding themselves that it is mostly muscle.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>So, is there a limit to the amount of body fat we should allow ourselves to gain when bulking? I’d argue so, yes. Cutting also. But these are not hard cut-off points, only guidelines.</p><p>[<strong>Note: </strong>Women carry ~8% more essential fat and should add 8% to all guidelines.]</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="801" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage-1024x801.png" alt="The Optimal Cut–Bulk Range for Body-fat Percentage" class="wp-image-59544" style="width:512px;height:401px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage-1024x801.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage-450x352.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage-768x601.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage-1536x1202.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage-1568x1227.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage.png 1624w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Optimal-Cut–Bulk-Range-for-Body-fat-Percentage-450x352@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption"><span style="color:#000000" class="tadv-color">Figure 10. Anabolic signaling tends to drop off under ~10% body fat and after ~20% body fat. This gives us the recommended range to stay between when <a href="https://rippedbody.com/bulk-cut-cycles/">cycling cut and bulk phases</a>.</span></figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <strong>I recommend that you don’t bulk past 20% body fat.</strong></p><p>It is unclear whether someone who bulks from 12% body fat will have a calorie partitioning advantage over someone who starts bulking at 18%, but there are clear aesthetic advantages. Also, importantly, as we move past 20%, there are increased health risks.</p><p>For that reason, I recommend that people cap their bulk cycles at 20% body fat. This means that I recommend people only start to bulk once they are below 15% body fat because otherwise, they won’t have enough uninterrupted time to bulk before they need to cut again.</p><div you don’t cut beyond 10% body fat if your intention is to bulk right after.</strong></p><p>The natural reaction to knowing that body fat will be gained in a bulking phase is that people tend to want to get as lean as possible prior to starting. But to attempt to keep your abs year-round is a common mistake.</p><p>If you wish to diet town to below 10% body fat and hang out there for a while, perhaps because you want to look your best for the summer season, that’s cool.</p><p>But if your intention with cutting is to only prepare yourself for the bulk, don’t go far past 10% body fat, as hormonal function (and therefore anabolic signaling) tends to be a little off, so your training efforts are likely to be in vain.</p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-gallery
