style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>Therefore, I recommend you keep their cut–bulk cycles between ~10 and 20% body fat.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> Here’s my full gallery of <a href="https://rippedbody.com/body-fat-guide/">body-fat percentage pictures</a>.</p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">8. EVERYTHING ELSE</h3><p>This includes the ratio of your fat-carb macronutrient intake for the remainder of your calorie budget after protein, micronutrients, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/nutrient-timing/" data-wpil-monitor-id="247">nutrient timing</a>, and supplements.)</p><p>The importance of each of these increases as we advance with our training and get closer to our genetic potential. See my free <a href="https://rippedbody.com/free-nutrition-course/">nutrition setup guide</a> for more.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><div style="height:50px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="how-quickly">PART 2: HOW QUICKLY WE CAN EXPECT TO GAIN MUSCLE WHEN BULKING UP</h2><p>So we know that our level of training advancement determines our rate of muscle growth potential, which decreases with experience. (Contrast this to when we have a fat-loss goal, where <a href="https://rippedbody.com/body-fat-guide/">body-fat percentage</a>  determines how quickly we can lose fat and has nothing to do with training experience.)</p><p>By categorizing our training advancement, we can get a reasonable estimate of the amount of muscle we can hope/expect to gain per month. This is useful to know when setting calorie intake and body weight gain targets so that we avoid gaining too quickly (and thus putting on an unnecessary amount of fat), or gaining more slowly than we otherwise could have.</p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">CLASSIFYING TRAINING EXPERIENCE</h3><p>Classifying training advancement is difficult, but I like to split it into the following four categories and define them as follows:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Beginner:</strong> New to serious training, first few months.</li><li><strong>Novice:</strong> Still able to progress most training loads on a week to week basis.</li><li><strong>Intermediate:</strong> Able to <a href="https://rippedbody.com/progression/">progress most training loads on a month-to-month basis</a>.</li><li><strong>Advanced:</strong> Progress is evident only when viewed over multiple months or a year.</li></ol><h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="musclegain">RATES OF MUSCLE GAIN POTENTIAL</h3><p>Here is a rough breakdown of the rate of muscle growth the average person can expect if they do everything right:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Beginner:</strong> 1–1.5% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Novice:</strong> 0.75–1.25% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Intermediate:</strong> 0.5–0.75% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Advanced:</strong> Less than 0.5% of body weight per month.</li></ol><p>We will use these numbers to help us set our monthly bodyweight gain targets, depending on which of the three ways we choose to bulk. (But as you will see, I only really recommend just the one.)</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><div style="height:50px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="ways">PART 3: THE THREE WAYS TO BULK UP</h2><p>Straight talk — I have never seen anyone gain a significant amount of muscle without also gaining fat.</p><p>The only exceptions to this are skinny, new trainees and those who have had a long lay-off from training where they lost a significant amount of muscle.</p><p>I say this because I want to be clear — you <em>will</em> gain some fat when you bulk. The only question is how much fat you will gain, which is determined by how fast you set your rate of weight gain.</p><p>I view there as being <s>three</s> two legitimate ways to successfully bulk, each with their pros and cons:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>The</strong> <strong>relaxed bulk</strong> aims to maximize the rate of muscle growth without requiring the counting of calories or macros. More fat is gained using this method, and it’s considered the acceptable trade-off for simplicity.</li><li><strong>The lean bulk</strong> aims to maximize levels of leanness, while still adding muscle mass. The changes are the hardest to measure, and I don’t recommend it.</li><li><strong>The controlled/slow bulk</strong> aims to maximize the rate of muscle growth while accepting some fat gain. This is the easiest to make clear progress while <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-adjust-macros-bulk/" data-wpil-monitor-id="110">minimizing fat gain</a>. This is what I recommend.</li></ul><p>For all options, the underlying assumption is that whether for appearance or athletic reasons, our goal is to build a more muscled version of ourselves<em>.</em> Whichever method is chosen, you’ll diet off any fat gained later.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">THE RELAXED BULK</h3><p><strong>The</strong> <strong>Relaxed Bulk</strong> aims to maximize the rate of muscle growth without requiring the counting of calories or macros. More fat is gained using this method, and it’s considered the acceptable trade-off for simplicity.</p><p>The rate of muscle growth can still be maximized, even if you don’t focus on counting macros, as long as you’re conscious of getting in enough protein and you eat enough.</p><p>You’ll aim to gain weight significantly faster than your potential to gain muscle (and the other two methods of bulking). Fat gain will be higher as a result, but you choose not to care due to the ease of it.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="787" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk-1024x787.png" alt="The Relaxed Bulking Method" class="wp-image-59562" style="width:512px;height:394px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk-1024x787.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk-450x346.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk-768x590.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk-1536x1180.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk-1568x1205.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk.png 1851w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Relaxed-Bulk-450x346@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 11. More fat is gained than muscle with a relaxed bulk, but you choose not to care due to the ease of it.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>A relaxed bulk should not be confused with a <em><a href="https://rippedbody.com/dreambulkeritis/">dream bulk</a> — </em>where people simply eat their faces off, gaining an enormous amount of weight and kidding themselves that it is mostly muscle. In contrast, when we use this <em>relaxed bulk</em> method we remain conscious of our maximal rates of muscle growth potential.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">HOW TO IMPLEMENT A RELAXED BULK</h4><p>I recommend the following rates of weight gain when executing a relaxed bulk:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Beginner & Novice Relaxed Bulk:</strong> ~3% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Intermediate Relaxed Bulk:</strong> ~2% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Advanced Relaxed Bulk:</strong> ~1% of body weight per month.</li></ol><p>Your calorie intake will be 2.0–2.5x your maximum muscular gain potential, which is sufficiently high enough that a lack of attention paid to the macros won’t compromise it, as you’re unlikely to fall short on any of them.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <strong>A 160 lb lean, novice trainee</strong> will aim to gain ~5 lbs per month (160 lbs * 3%)</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <strong>A 180 lb intermediate trainee</strong> will aim to gain ~3.5 lbs per month (180 lbs * 2%)</p><p>If you gain more than your target rate of weight gain, reduce your food intake. If you gain less than the target, increase your food intake. If you find yourself struggling to auto-regulate like this without counting, then consider starting to count as per the controlled bulk method.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="lean-bulk">THE (THEORETICAL) LEAN BULK</h3><p>The <em>lean bulk</em> aims to maximize levels of leanness, while still adding muscle mass.</p><p>Theoretically, this can be achieved by making increases to calorie and macro intake <em>only</em> enough to allow progressions with our training (indicating muscle gain). In practice, I haven’t seen anyone achieve this without losing their minds because of the slow rate of progression, which makes it exceptionally hard to manage.</p><p>We know that we cannot maximize muscle growth rate with this method, but I can’t say for sure what percentage of the maximal growth rate we can achieve on average. I suspect half or less, which means you will aim to gain weight at less than 1/3 the rate of the other methods.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="787" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk-1024x787.png" alt="The Hypothetical Lean Bulk" class="wp-image-59561" style="width:512px;height:394px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk-1024x787.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk-450x346.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk-768x590.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk-1536x1180.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk-1568x1205.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk.png 1851w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/The-Hypothetical-Lean-Bulk-450x346@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 12. The total rate of weight change with the Lean Bulk is so slow that it’s hard to manage. It’s theoretically possible, but in practice, very hard to achieve.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>For this reason, it requires a lot more patience as the changes are <em>far</em> more subtle, and in the short term, you may not be able to measure them. This requires faith in the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/principle-progressive-overload/" data-wpil-monitor-id="111">principle that if you are progressing with your training</a>, then you are gaining muscle, but most people cannot do this without losing their minds.</p><p>This style is only really suitable for experienced trainees, that place a premium on maximal leanness, and are very confident that they know what they are doing with their training and diet. This is something that the professional figure model needs to do to stay employed.</p><p>From my point of view as a coach, this does not lend itself well to coaching because the changes are often too subtle to keep people motivated and feel value for their money. The reasons for this will become evident when we get to the comparison graphs section below.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">HOW TO IMPLEMENT A LEAN BULK</h4><p>You need to start from a <a href="https://rippedbody.com/body-fat-guide/">low body fat</a> to use this method. This is because you will be using a combination of the photos, stomach measurements, and body-fat calipers (a case where I recommend these) to gauge whether you have gained fat or muscle.</p><p>If you have been dieting (which you probably will have) then:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>Add calories to bring yourself to <a href="https://rippedbody.com/finding-calorie-maintenance/" data-wpil-monitor-id="112">maintenance</a> calorie intake.</li><li>Make small increases of 100–150 kcal (scaling with body size) to daily calorie intake when your training fails to progress.</li></ol><p>If you haven’t been dieting and your body weight is stable, then just skip the first part of this.</p><p>If you are currently gaining weight at more than the expected rate of muscle growth, then consider cutting back calorie intake slightly.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">LEAN BULK EXAMPLE ADJUSTMENTS</h4><p>Imagine you’re an advanced trainee, 180 lbs, 10% body fat, have just finished dieting, and you were losing 0.75 lbs per week. Here’s how you’d go about transitioning into the lean bulk.</p><p>1. It takes a 500 kcal daily deficit to lose 1 lb per week. So, add back in 375 kcal. (0.75 *500)</p><p>2. Make this increase via your preferred mix of carbs and fats. So, 10 g of fat and 70 g of carbs, for example.</p><p>3. Wait and track things over a couple of weeks. If you feel more energetic in the gym and training starts improving, but you see that your weight is still dropping <em>slightly</em>, make another increase. This time you increase by ~200 kcal, 5 g of fat, and 40 g of carbs.</p><p>4. Over the next couple of weeks, strength starts improving. But your weight has come up by three pounds! Is it fat? The photos and body-fat caliper measurements show no difference, but the stomach measurements have increased. This tells you that there is no fat gain, just an increase in glycogen, gut content, and water. Don’t change your macros.</p><p>5. Four weeks later, your <a href="https://rippedbody.com/progression/" data-wpil-monitor-id="113">training progression</a> has stalled. Body-fat appears unchanged. Make another increase, this time ~150 kcal, adding 5 g of fat, 25 g of carbs.</p><p>Keep on making fine-tune adjustments to your macros like this to keep progressing with your training without any fat gain.</p><p>This is theoretical. I haven’t seen someone succeed in practice. And before you think you’re the exception and go ahead anyway, part four will show why this method will lead to slower physique progress over the years than the controlled bulk, even if you manage to implement it successfully.</p><p>Trust me, I wish we could all stay lean year-round, but it just isn’t realistic to do so and make meaningful progress.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">THE CONTROLLED/SLOW BULK</h3><p>The controlled bulk aims to maximize the rate of muscle growth while accepting some fat gain.</p><p>Of the three methods we’re covering in this article, this is the one I use with <a href="https://rippedbody.com/coaching/" data-wpil-monitor-id="114">coaching clients</a> because it leads to the clearest signs of progress without leaving them with a lot of fat to cut off after the bulk. (It’s the controlled bulk method for which I have set the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/">macro calculator</a>.)</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="787" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks-1024x787.png" alt="How Controlled Bulking Looks" class="wp-image-59531" style="width:512px;height:394px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks-1024x787.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks-450x346.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks-768x590.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks-1536x1180.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks-1568x1205.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks.png 1851w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/How-a-Controlled-Bulk-looks-450x346@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 12. Gaining muscle and fat in a 1:1 ratio is a realistic outcome when you get a controlled bulk right.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h4 class="wp-block-heading" id="weightgain">TARGET RATES OF WEIGHT GAIN FOR THE CONTROLLED BULK</h4><p>I recommend the following rates of weight gain when executing a controlled bulk:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Beginner Controlled Bulk:</strong> 2% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Novice <strong>Controlled Bulk</strong>:</strong> 1.5% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Intermediate <strong>Controlled Bulk</strong>:</strong> 1% of body weight per month.</li><li><strong>Advanced <strong>Controlled Bulk</strong>:</strong> 0.5% of body weight per month.</li></ol><p>We know that some fat will be gained when we bulk; therefore, we know that to achieve our maximal rate of muscle growth, we have to gain weight at a higher rate than that. But how much higher should we set our rates of body weight gain? Research can’t tell us this, but experience coaching people leaves clues on what is appropriate.</p><p>Over the years, the average client has gained muscle and fat in a 1:1 ratio. Sometimes the results are a little better, and sometimes they are a little worse. I’m fairly sure the differences between clients are down to genetic luck rather than effort, as I tend to work with people who truly give it their all.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">HOW TO IMPLEMENT A CONTROLLED BULK</h4><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>Step 1:</strong> Calculate the initial calorie adjustment.</li><li><strong>Step 2:</strong> Adjust your macros and track progress over five weeks.</li><li><strong>Step 3:</strong> Adjust calorie and macro intake, if necessary.</li><li><strong>Step 4:</strong> Track progress. Adjust calorie intake again when necessary.</li></ul><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <a href="https://rippedbody.com/calories/">It takes roughly ~2500 kcal to build 1 lb of muscle and ~3500 kcal to burn or store 1 lb of fat</a>.</p><p>As people typically gain fat and muscle in a 1:1 ratio in a bulk phase, and if we assume a 30 day month, this means we need a 100 kcal daily caloric surplus to gain 1 lb of weight per month (~220 kcal for 1 kg).</p><p>However, just as the body has mechanisms to frustrate our dieting efforts (metabolic downregulation) in an attempt to ward off starvation, there are mechanisms that kick in to combat a calorie surplus. These aren’t as powerful as when we are dieting (historically, we were far less likely to eat ourselves to death than starve to death), but they vary from individual to individual.</p><p>A big one is called NEAT (non-exercise activity thermogenesis). It is the energy expended for everything we do that is not sleeping, eating, or sports-like exercise.</p><p>Just as some people experience more lethargy than others when dieting, dropping their NEAT, some people get more active than others when bulking (they start fidgeting more throughout the day, for example). This is impossible to predict, but I suggest we add 50%, which gives the following heuristic:</p><p><strong><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"></strong> <strong>To gain 1 lb of weight per month, add 150 kcal each day (330 kcal for 1 kg).</strong></p><p>NEAT, along with hunger-fullness signaling differences, explains why some people have a hard time gaining weight. You’ll see them referred to as ‘hardgainers.’ There’s nothing wrong; they just have to eat more or ignore hunger signals more.</p><p>Additionally, those who have been dieting prior will find that they can eat more than just the calculated energy difference of their weekly fat-loss rate as their hormones come back into balance, and metabolisms speed back up to normal.</p><p><em>This means that there isn’t a single calculation we can do to set our calorie intake perfectly for the duration of a bulk.</em> We need to track how the scale weight changes over time and adjust continually, hence why this is a multi-step process.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Controlled BULK EXAMPLE ADJUSTMENTS</h4><h5 class="wp-block-heading">Step 1: The initial calorie And Macro adjustment</h5><p>If you haven’t been tracking your weight or calorie intake up until now, just use <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macro-calculator/">the macro calculator</a> and then <a href="https://rippedbody.com/diet-progress-tracking/">track your progress like this</a> for 5 weeks.</p><p>If you have, we have the opportunity to be a little more accurate as we can base the adjustments off of your data. I’ll use two examples to explain how to do this.</p><p><strong>1) A 160 lb lean, novice trainee, currently not gaining or losing any weight.</strong></p><p>As he is not currently gaining or losing weight, he is at approximately maintenance calories. We will target a weight gain of 1.5% per month, which is 2.4 lbs.</p><p>Therefore, he needs to increase daily caloric intake by <em>360 kcal per day</em> (2.4 lbs * 150 kcal).</p><p><strong>2) A 180 lb intermediate trainee, finished his cut to shreds, recently losing 1.0 lbs per week.</strong></p><p>He is currently in an approximate 500 kcal daily deficit and will target a weight gain of 1% per month, which is 1.8 lbs.</p><p>Therefore, he needs to increase daily caloric intake by 770<em> kcal per day </em>(500 + 1.8 * 150).</p><figure class="wp-block-image"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="827" height="514" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Ben-Carpenter-Controlled-Bulk-Slow-Bulk.jpg" alt="Ben Carpenter Transformation - Bulking up in a controlled way" class="wp-image-39376" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Ben-Carpenter-Controlled-Bulk-Slow-Bulk.jpg 827w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Ben-Carpenter-Controlled-Bulk-Slow-Bulk-450x280.jpg 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Ben-Carpenter-Controlled-Bulk-Slow-Bulk-768x477.jpg 768w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 827px) 100vw, 827px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">My buddy, <a href="https://www.instagram.com/bdccarpenter/?hl=en" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Ben Carpenter’s</a> ‘Slow Bulk’. ~20 lb gain in body weight – some of which will simply be from the glycogen & water regain aside from the muscle & fat mass increases.</figcaption></figure><h5 class="wp-block-heading">STEP 2: ADJUST YOUR MACROS and TRACK PROGRESS OVER FIVE WEEKS</h5><p>So this brings us on the macronutrients — how much of each should we increase to reach these new calorie requirements?</p><p><strong><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"></strong> <strong>I recommend you leave protein as it is and increase your carb and fat intake in a 3:1 ratio.</strong></p><p>A 1 lb increase of muscle per month would bump up our protein requirements by ~1g. That is a minor amount, and I don’t think it’s worth adjusting for.</p><p>For carbs and fats, if I look back through the data with clients, what seems to have worked best on average is something like an approximate 75–25 split in favor of carbs. When I wrote <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://muscleandstrengthpyramids.com/" target="_blank">The Muscle and Strength Pyramid: Nutrition</a> book with Eric Helms, this jived with his experience also, and we recommend fat intakes in the 20–30% of total calorie intake range when at maintenance or bulking, 15-25% when cutting.</p><p>Recall from the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/free-nutrition-course/">Nutrition Setup Guide</a> and <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-count-macros/">How To Make Meals Out Of Your Macros</a> guide that 1 g of protein and carbohydrate = ~4 kcal, 1 g of fat = ~9 kcal.</p><p><strong>1) Our novice trainee needs a 360 kcal increase per day.</strong></p><p>He doesn’t have a strong preference for carbs vs. fats, so he splits things 75% carbs, 25% fats.</p><p>This gives him a daily increase of <em>10 g fat, ~65 g carbs</em>. (10 * 9 kcal + 67.5 * 4 kcal = 360 kcal)</p><p><strong>2) Our intermediate trainee needs a 770 kcal increase per day.</strong></p><p>As he’s been dieting, fat intake likely had to be cut down to the lower end of the recommended range to keep carb intake high enough to support the training. Now we can push this back up. Therefore, we’ll skew the first calorie increase a little more toward fat.</p><p>This gives him a daily increase of 25 <em>g fat, 135 g carbs</em>. (25 * 9 kcal + 136.25 * 4 kcal = 770 kcal)</p><h5 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>STEP 3:</strong> ADJUST CALORIE INTAKE, IF NECESSARY</h5><p>Take a look at your average rate of weight change over the last five weeks. Ignore the change that happened in the first week and calculate the average rate of weight change per week over the last four weeks.</p><p><strong><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"></strong> Add or subtract 150 kcal per pound (330 kcal/ kg) you are off your monthly target to your calorie intake each day.</p><p>Let’s consider what this might look like for our two trainees.</p><p><strong>1) Here’s the scale weight data for our 160 lb novice trainee, aiming to gain 2.4 lbs per month, since the calorie and macro change:</strong></p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>160 » 162 lbs</li><li>162.4 lbs</li><li>162.9 lbs</li><li>163.2 lbs</li><li>163.4 lbs</li></ol><p>We ignore the first week’s bump, which will have muscle glycogen, gut content, and water in the mix.</p><p>The change from the end of week 1 to the end of week 5 has been 1.4 lbs. This is 1 lb short of the monthly target, so he needs to <em>add 150 kcal</em> to his daily calorie intake. He does that by adding <em>25 g of carbs</em>,<em> 5 g of fat</em>.</p><p><strong>2) Here’s the scale weight data for our 180 lb intermediate trainee, aiming to gain 1.8 lbs per month, since the calorie and macro change:</strong></p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>180 » 186.1 lbs</li><li>186.3 lbs</li><li>186.5 lbs</li><li>186.8 lbs</li><li>186.9 lbs</li></ol><p>You can see there is a bigger bump in weight in the first week for our intermediate trainee because this calorie increase was bigger. We’ll ignore this for the same reasons also.</p><p>The change from the end of week 1 to the end of week 5 has been 0.8 lbs. This is also 1 lb short of the monthly target, so he needs to <em>add 150 kcal</em> to his daily calorie intake. He does that by adding ~<em>25 g of carbs,</em><em> 5 g of fats</em>.</p><h5 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>STEP 4:</strong> ADJUST CALORIE INTAKE AGAIN WHEN NECESSARY</h5><p>Repeat step 3 every five weeks and adjust if necessary. I don’t recommend that you adjust more frequently than this — the changes when bulking are small, the scale <a href="/holiday-weight-gain" data-wpil-monitor-id="115">weight can fluctuate</a>, it requires a longer time period then dieting to tease out true changes from noise.</p><p>Here’s how this might all look:</p><p><strong>Our 160 lb lean, novice trainee’s first serious bulk</strong></p><figure class="wp-block-table"><table><tbody><tr><td><strong>Schedule</strong></td><td><strong>Initial macros (pcf)</strong></td><td><strong>1st adjustment</strong></td><td><strong>2nd adjustment</strong></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Training day</strong></td><td>160/455/70</td><td>160/520/80</td><td>160/555/85</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Rest day</strong></td><td>160/260/90</td><td>160/325/100</td><td>160/350/105</td></tr></tbody></table></figure><p><strong>Our 180 lb intermediate trainee, bulking after dieting to shreds</strong></p><figure class="wp-block-table"><table><tbody><tr><td><strong>Schedule</strong></td><td><strong>End of diet macros</strong></td><td><strong>1st adjustment</strong></td><td><strong>2nd adjustment</strong></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Training day</strong></td><td>180/250/40</td><td>180/385/65</td><td>180/410/70</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Rest day</strong></td><td>180/100/60</td><td>180/235/85</td><td>180/260/90</td></tr></tbody></table></figure><p>The intermediate trainee will likely have to make a few further adjustments as their metabolic rate comes back up to normal levels after the diet.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><div style="height:50px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="comparisons">PART 4: HOW TO BULK UP — COMPARING FAT AND MUSCLE GAIN WITH DIFFERENT METHODS</h2><p>If I didn’t send you to sleep halfway through reading this, you’ll know that the method I recommend is the controlled bulk. Here are some comparisons of different scenarios justifying that position. I am assuming the same starting level of leanness for all.</p><p>Ultimately, you need to choose a method based on your <em>effort</em>-reward preference. It needs to be a method that you can be consistent with, and this will come down to the tug of your social life vs. satisfaction derived from staying lean or seeing progress.</p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">1. BULKING PHASE COMPARISONS</h3><p>Imagine three identical triplets decide to do each of the three methods. They are all starting from the same ‘shredded’ level of leanness. Here is how a 6-month bulk phase would look:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="818" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527-1024x818.png" alt="Bulking phase muscle building vs fat gain comparisons" class="wp-image-59556" style="width:512px;height:409px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527-1024x818.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527-450x359.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527-768x613.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527-1536x1226.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527-1568x1252.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527.png 1776w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-gaining-phase-comparisons-e1589434806527-450x359@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 13. A comparison of the gaining phase for the three bulking methods.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><p>By the 6-month mark, the <em>relaxed</em> and <em>controlled bulk</em> methods have resulted in the same amount of total muscle gain and are clearly ahead of the<em> lean bulk</em> method.</p><p>However, looking at this 6-month snapshot is a false comparison; we are going to want to lose the fat that is gained after we have bulked. So, let’s extend the time frame to include the cut phase and see what happens to our three identical triplets.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em><strong>Muscle Mass Increases:</strong> Slow bulk = Relaxed bulk > Lean gains.</em></p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">2. Cut Comparison When There Is A Sudden Deadline To Get Shredded</h3><p>In this first example, I’ll assume that there is a sudden deadline (competition or photoshoot) set for our guys two months out. They <em>have</em> to get back down to baseline leanness — that shredded state they had before — or they lose their jobs, sponsorship, etc. Here’s how that would look:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="707" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-1024x707.png" alt="Cut comparison given a sudden deadline" class="wp-image-59559" style="width:512px;height:354px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-1024x707.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-450x311.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-768x530.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-1536x1060.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-2048x1413.png 2048w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-1568x1082.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-sudden-deadline-e1589434721335-450x311@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 14. A comparison of overall lean mass levels after a sudden 2-month deadline is imposed.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>The <em>controlled bulk</em> guy has time to get back down to base-level leanness, with minimal lean mass losses.</li><li>The <em>relaxed bulk</em> guy has to rush his cut; he loses more and more lean mass as the weeks progress. This happens because he has to force his body to lose fat at a rate higher than it can do so without muscle losses.</li><li>The <em>lean bulk</em> guy does not need to cut; he continues to gain mass very steadily.</li></ul><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em><strong>Net Muscle Mass Increases:</strong> Slow bulk > Lean gains > Relaxed bulk.</em></p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">3. Cut Comparison when a Deadline Is Known In Advance</h3><p>Alright, so you’re going to call me out on the previous example and say that this doesn’t apply to most people, that people will not rush their cut and lose muscle mass. I’d argue that the majority of people make this mistake (setting false deadlines for themselves and wasting their hard-earned gains), but we, being educated about these things, are not them. Fair enough.</p><p>So, let’s say you have a deadline a long way in advance — a long beach holiday, for example — and you want to look your best by that point. Here’s how that would look:</p><figure class="wp-block-image size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="604" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-1024x604.png" alt="Cut comparison given a known deadline" class="wp-image-59558" style="width:512px;height:302px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-1024x604.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-450x265.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-768x453.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-1536x906.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-2048x1208.png 2048w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-1568x925.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Cut-comparison-given-a-known-deadline-e1589434748280-450x265@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 15. A comparison of the overall lean mass levels when the deadline is known in advance.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>The situation is the same as the previous example for the <em>controlled bulk</em> and <em>lean bulk</em> guy. The former can cut at the same time; the latter can continue until his holiday.</li><li>The <em>relaxed bulk</em> guy has to start his cut much earlier. His bulk phase is shorter than the <em>slow-bulk</em> guy so the muscle gain is lower, but he achieves a greater overall gain than the <em>lean gains</em> guy.</li></ul><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em><strong>Net Muscle Mass Increases:</strong> Slow bulk > Relaxed bulk > Lean bulk.</em></p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">4. WHAT HAPPENS WHEN PEOPLE CUT FOR SUMMER</h3><p>Ok, so let’s say that there is no deadline to get shredded, and both the <em>controlled bulk</em> and <em>relaxed bulk</em> methods start their cut at the same time.</p><p>Now, before you hammer me for being nonsensical, I’d point out that this is what most people do: bulk for the majority of the year and then cut for the summer period, starting the same time each year (usually May), <em>irrespective</em> of how much body fat they are carrying. This is because they don’t want to compromise their ‘off-season’ bulk time frame, and is usually accompanied by complaints that they never seem to be able to get shredded in time for summer.</p><p>Here’s how that looks assuming they don’t rush the cut to the point muscle mass is lost any more than normal:</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="664" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-1024x664.png" alt="Summer cut comparison" class="wp-image-59560" style="width:512px;height:332px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-1024x664.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-450x292.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-768x498.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-1536x996.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-2048x1329.png 2048w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-1568x1017.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Summer-cut-comparison-e1589434692833-450x292@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 16. A comparison of the overall lean mass levels when people follow the typical path of getting ready for summer.</figcaption></figure><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>The <em>lean bulk</em> guy does not need to cut; he can continue to grow throughout summer gradually.</li><li>It takes the <em>relaxed bulk</em> guy until the end of summer to get to maximal leanness, but he feels far too fat at the start, and this affects his enjoyment at the beach, possibly choosing not to go for the first month of summer.</li><li>The <em>slow bulk</em> guy gets to maximal leanness by early-mid summer and can seek to maintain that condition, or move on into a slow-bulk phase. If the slow bulk phase is started immediately upon hitting maximal leanness (as depicted in the graph above), there will be a small amount of fat regain, but arguably low enough to remain in good beach condition before the end of the summer. This is worth considering if you wish to maximize your gains in the coming year as it will give you a longer bulking period.</li></ul><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em><strong>Net Muscle Mass Increases:</strong> Slow bulk > Relaxed bulk > Lean bulk.</em></p><h3 class="wp-block-heading">5. WHAT HAPPENS WHEN Setting AN UPPER LIMIT TO BODY-FAT PERCENTAGE</h3><p>Imagine that you wish to bulk but don’t want to go past a certain level of body fat so that you can stay in year-round good condition. This way, you will feel comfortable taking your shirt off at any time.</p><p>Let’s call this 15% body fat — a level where with enough muscle mass, you will still look good. (15% body fat when you are weak and carry very little muscle doesn’t.) You will bulk until you hit 15% and then start your cut. Thus, to the outsider, you will always look somewhere between good condition and great condition.</p><p>It is difficult to assess body-fat percentage accurately using the methods we have available, so I would recommend using a combination of my <a href="https://rippedbody.com/body-fat-guide/">visual guide to body-fat percentage</a>, and the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-calculate-body-fat-percentage/">US Navy Method of Body-fat Estimation</a>.</p><p>Here’s how the three different methods of bulking compare over a longer period:</p><figure class="wp-block-image alignwide size-full"><a href="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/03/SLOW-BULK-VS.-RELAXED-BULK-LONG-TERM-GROWTH-COMPARISON-STAYING-UNDER-15-PERCENT.jpg"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="4018" height="1638" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766.png" alt="Bulk long-term growth comparisons" class="wp-image-59557" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766.png 4018w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766-450x183.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766-1024x417.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766-768x313.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766-1536x626.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766-2048x835.png 2048w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766-1568x639.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Bulk-long-term-growth-comparisons-e1589434773766-450x183@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 4018px) 100vw, 4018px"></a><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Figure 17: Comparing the three different bulking strategies, you can see that the controlled bulk leads to the most muscle mass gain after two bulk-cut cycles. Frankly, I think I’ve been a little generous with the relaxed bulk here and the outcome is likely worse.</figcaption></figure><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>For the lean bulk, with no cutting period, gains can be made throughout.</li><li>The <em>slow bulk</em> method allows us to have longer bulking periods and more time spent growing overall, which leads to greater muscle mass increases.</li><li>With the <em>relaxed bulk</em> method, bulking periods have to be shorter because the cut phase has to start sooner. Thus the overall time spent gaining muscle is shorter, and less muscle mass gained.</li></ul><p>Over longer time periods (in the above we have ~17 months), the <em>slow bulk</em> guy makes more progress towards their maximum muscular potential than the other two methods.</p><p>Note how the <em>lean bulk</em> approach is not far behind the relaxed bulk in terms of growth over longer time frames in this situation. However, I just want to emphasize – due to the patience that it requires and the subtlety of the changes people often lose their minds before being successful with the <em>lean bulk</em> method of bulking.</p><p>Thus, with the exclusion of special populations, when it comes to client work, I find myself recommending and guiding people through controlled bulks.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449-1f3fb.svg"> <em><strong>Long-Term Net Muscle Mass Increases:</strong> Slow bulk > Relaxed bulk > Lean gains.</em></p><div style="height:20px" aria-hidden="true" class="wp-block-spacer"></div><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><a href="https://rippedbody.com/adjustments-manual/"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="634" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1024x634.png" alt="The Diet Adjustments Manual by Andy Morgan - Device Display" class="wp-image-60161" style="width:768px;height:476px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1024x634.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-450x278.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1536x950.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display-1568x970.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Diet_Adjustments_Manual_-_Device_Display.png 2041w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></a></figure><p>Thank you for reading. To learn more about adjustments for bulking, maintenance, cutting, and the transition phases between them, check out my book, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/adjustments-manual/"><strong>The Diet Adjustments Manual</strong>.</a></p><p>Questions welcomed in the comments. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🙏🏻" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f64f-1f3fb.svg"><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="❤️" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/2764.svg"></p><p>– Andy</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">How To Bulk Q&A</h3><div class="schema-faq wp-block-yoast-faq-block"><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596696633755"><strong class="schema-faq-question">What should I eat to bulk?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">The foods you should eat to bulk are the same as those when you cut; you’ll simply have more of them and room for ‘treats.’<br><br>Your diet should be rich in fruits, vegetables, and have sufficient protein (<a href="https://rippedbody.com/macros/">1 g/lb of body weight per day</a>). The rest should be made up of predominantly fresh and unprocessed foods. These things will help keep you full when dieting, help ensure muscle mass retention, and cover your micronutritional needs.<br><br>This advice does not change when you bulk. However, because you have a higher calorie budget, you can fit in calorie-dense treats that you might crave from time to time. These should not come at the cost of nutrient-dense foods (fruits and vegetables).<br><br>Most clients eat the same kind of diet year-round; they just increase or decrease their carb intake and frequency that they eat out. I like to have a bowl of cereal with a chocolate protein shake made with skimmed milk to meet my carb requirements. Here’s my <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-count-macros/">guide to making meals out of your macros</a>.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596696659281"><strong class="schema-faq-question">How can I bulk up fast?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Most answers to the question of how to bulk up fast focus on foods people should eat. But the simple fact of the matter is that there is no way to bulk up faster than your genetic potential. If you do, you’re just getting fat.<br><br>Recall from the start of this guide that how fast you can grow depends on your genetics, calorie intake, training quality, sufficient protein intake, sleep quality, and stress levels. Make sure you take each of these seriously. Refer to the <em>How Quickly Can We Gain Muscle As We Bulk?</em> section, so that you know what rate of weight gain to target. This will stop you from progressing more slowly than you otherwise could, but more commonly, getting fat.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596696671178"><strong class="schema-faq-question">Are bananas good for bulking?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">The average 125 g banana has 27 g of carbs with just 3 g of fiber. This means they’re relatively high in carbs and low in fiber, so you’re unlikely to poop yourself if you eat a lot of bananas to hit your carb requirements (unlike with other fruits).<br><br>However, roughly half of the carbs will be fructose content. Fructose is processed differently to the glucose you get from the carbs in rice, potatoes, bread, and pasta. So while it would be fine to eat several bananas per day, it’s probably not optimal to rely on them exclusively to meet your carbohydrate needs.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596696686853"><strong class="schema-faq-question">Is rice good for bulking up?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Yes, rice is good for bulking up but no better than pasta, bread, potatoes, or other starchy sources to meet your carb requirements. Feel free to choose what you enjoy and make sure you don’t skimp on protein intake, of the other 9 critical factors for bulking up that I cover in this guide.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596696710321"><strong class="schema-faq-question">Where can I find out about macros, meal timing, and supplements?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">These are covered in my <a href="https://rippedbody.com/complete-diet-nutrition-set-up-guide/">Nutrition Setup Guide</a>.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596696734495"><strong class="schema-faq-question">How should I train?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Click the training tab in the menu to see a list of sample programs from my <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://muscleandstrengthpyramids.com/" target="_blank">Muscle and Strength Training Pyramid book</a>.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1596696759116"><strong class="schema-faq-question">What should I do when I want to cut?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Here’s my <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-adjust-macros/">guide for macro adjustments when cutting</a>. But if this topic interests you, check out my book: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/adjustments-manual/"><strong>The Diet Adjustments Manual</strong></a>.</p></div></div></div><form action="https://app.kit.com/forms/7749683/subscriptions" style="background-color:#fffefa;border-radius:10px" class="seva-form formkit-form" method="post" data-sv-form="7749683" data-uid="8462769b72" data-format="inline" data-version="5" min-width="400 500 600 700"><div data-style="full" style="--bg-border-radius:5px"><div data-element="column" class="formkit-column"><div class="formkit-header" style="color:#333333;font-weight:700" data-element="header"><h2><strong>STOP GUESSING. ⠀
For the majority of my 20s, I fell for the scams of the fitness industry. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🤦🏻‍♂️" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f926-1f3fb-200d-2642-fe0f.svg">
⠀
This site is my revenge. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="😬" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f62c.svg">
⠀
Since 2011, I’ve written guides explaining how we get results to help readers avoid the same path of frustration. We’ve helped over 2,000 busy working guys transform their bodies WITHOUT supplements, fad diets, or the process dominating their lives.

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>I have been following your newsletter and website for quite a while and have a question about bulking. I am training for calisthenics, and wanted to put on more muscle. I am 5 ft 7 and weigh 135 lbs. Estimated BF% around 14% (waist 30″, neck 13″).</p>
<p>I have always wanted to lean bulk, been doing that for 6 months and seeing slow results. (Weekly average weight fluctuates <0.5lb but I was able to progress with calisthenics skills). I would like to try the controlled/slow bulk method, but afraid that the additional fat gain would affect my calisthenics skills. Your training program consists of bodybuilding and powerlifting. </p>
<p>Do you have any suggestions for bulking up with calisthenics training?</p>

</div>

    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-90992">
        Brian L.
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Brian,</p>
<p>If you’re struggling to gain muscle at a satisfactory rate (measurable, manageable, motivational) with the “lean bulk,” then you’re right to switch to the slow/controlled bulk.</p>
<p>You will gain some fat and with the muscle. This is unavoidable.<br>
This extra weight may negatively affect some of your body weight (calisthenics) training, meaning that you can’t quite do as many reps or have the same level of endurance. (I say “may” because you’ll be gaining muscle and strength, which will offset this.)</p>
<p>But this is the price you have to be willing to pay to make progress. Otherwise, you’ll stay in physique purgatory.</p>
<p>Make sense?</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Amanda, thank you. Glad you’ve been finding my content helpful!</p>
<p>You bulked, you felt fat. You then cut but stopped because you felt you hadn’t gained enough muscle when bulking to be happy when you leaned out. You’re now considering bulking.</p>
<p>You can see how this could potentially go in cycles unless you change something, right? Exceptionally common. I call it physique purgatory.</p>
<p>Not training hard enough may have been a problem, and you may get a better result from your bulk. But ask yourself this: could you gain 8 lbs of fat and 15 lbs overall over the course of the next eight months and not want to quit before because you feel too fat? If not, get leaner first.</p>
<p>(And <a href="https://rippedbody.com/is-it-better-to-forget-about-body-fat-percentage/" rel="ugc">don’t trust that DEXA scan</a>.)</p>

</div>

    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-bulk/#comment-84567"></i></span>
</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hello Andy,<br>
wanted to ask if you had read the book "Glucose revolution" and if you think its guidelines are OK to be followed by someone who wants to have a ripped body <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🙂" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f642.svg"></p>
<p>The author recommends to eat in this exact order: fiber first, then protein and fats, after that carbs, and only eventually, sugars.</p>
<p>Do you think that eating in this way might be counterproductive (in terms of how the body will use the macros it receives with the food) or you don't see any issue? Or maybe you also suggest to follow this order?</p>
<p>Best regards</p>

    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="December 31, 2022 16:16">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>

</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-bulk/#comment-84571"></i></span>
</div>

    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-84567">
        George
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>1. I haven't, but with a title like that, I don't need to. It'll be a book spreading fear of carbohydrate intake, particularly sugar, using the carbohydrate-insulin model of obesity as justification. This theorizes that diets high in carbohydrates are particularly fattening due to their propensity to elevate insulin secretion. This is over-simplistic. You can ignore what you have read.</p>
<p>2. Eating in that order wouldn't be counterproductive, but it's unnecessary.</p>
<p>3. It is a good idea to prioritize lean meats, fruits, vegetables, dairy, nuts, and legumes (not an exhaustive list). It is not necessary to avoid sugar, but if you are trying to sustain a calorie deficit, it is best to minimize very highly processed foods, many of which will have a high sugar content (and a high-fat content), and not be very filling. When bulking, you still want to prioritize those foods I said, but if/when you struggle to eat enough to continue to gain weight, don't be afraid to eat more of those highly palatable foods.</p>
<p>Download my nutrition setup guide if you haven't already and read that. It'll give you all you need. The box to download it is at the end of the article.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Thank you very much for the answer Andy.</p>
<p>Just so you know, by reading that book I didn't get the impression that I should avoid/limit carbs or sugars, just that (for the insulinic response you already mentioned) it should be better to eat them after fibers, proteins, and fats…</p>
<p>(there're no limits on their quantity, it's just a matter of changing the order in which someone eats what s/he already does)</p>
<p>Also, I'm not that much interested in the fat loss aspect here, I'm trying to follow such order mainly for all the other healthy reasons that apparently are related with eating carbs/sugars at the end of the meal</p>
<p>Thank you again, appreciate you continuous contributions!</p>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy,</p>
<p>Currently in my first 5 weeks of a bulk. Confused about some scale changes since starting.</p>
<p>Average weekly weights:<br>
W1: 149.2<br>
W2: 150.3<br>
W3: 152<br>
W4: 152<br>
W5: 153.6</p>
<p>5’9 male, eating 2500 cals (as rec. by macro calculator), 7-8.5 hours sleep nightly, 0.7-0.9 g/lb protein, mod-high life stress. Following intermediate bodybuilding routine.</p>
<p>Not much scale movement in first 2 weeks, then suddenly a big “bump” in week 3. Is this glycogen, gut content, etc.? Wondering from which week onwards I should be tracking my monthly gain? Do you recommend keeping calories/macros the same for another 5 weeks?</p>
<p>Thank you,</p>
<p>Thomas</p>

You need to wait longer until the trend is clear. <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-adjust-macros-bulk/">Here’s my decision framework for adjusting calories and macros when bulking</a>.</p>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hey Andy! If someone only ate at maintenance and in a calorie deficit for years but worked his way up to benching 38 kg dumbbells for six reps and chin-ups with bodyweight + 20 kg for 6 reps without gaining much muscle, could he still get ‘beginners muscle gains’ if he ate in a calorie surplus?</p>

<p>Erik</p>

    </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Possibly yes, probably not.</p>
<p>Muscle is the engine that drives the potential to be strong. More muscle doesn’t necessarily mean more strength, but there is a limit to how strong we can be without more muscle gain. So, the muscle gain (and fat loss) may have already happened, but it was over too slow of a time scale to truly appreciate it.</p>
<p>I would set your weight gain target as an intermediate trainee would.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy – I’m a 27 Yo male, 84kg’s and 5’10</p>
<p>I’ve repeatedly been trying to lose weight on 1400 kcalories. My monthly average has been 1567 since March of this year and my weight has stayed relatively constant only losing 2kg, but my strength has increased. I’m tracking everything down to my cod liver oil – my macro average over the same period is <span>P 127g, C 168g, F 47g.</span></p>
<p>I estimate my current BF % in the range of 20 – 25%. I’m loathe to cut calories further to reduce my body fat but am also terrified of the increased fat gain associated with bulking at higher calories. I was wondering if you had any advice as to which direction I’d be best off moving in? Thank you!</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Fat and muscle mass don’t change suddenly. So this was some combination of a change in water, glycogen, or gut content.</p>
<p>You’ll see in my <a href="https://rippedbody.com/diet-progress-tracking/">Progress Tracking Guide</a> that I recommend people weigh each day and then average it over the course of the week. If you do that you’ll get used to these fluctuations. If you don’t, your data will be useless.</p>
<p>Now you may be wondering, <em>“Oh, Andy saw this comment but didn’t answer my other one a few weeks ago. Why?”</em></p>
<p>I deleted it. I’ve become frustrated and fed up with answering you. You’ve been asking questions since May last year but it’s become very clear that you haven’t read the guides on the site that I’ve been linking you to. You’re a ship at sea without a sail and I’m not going to watch it smash on the rocks any longer. Read the site. Read the other comments. It has what you need. Best of luck.</p>

    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-83575">
        Sim
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Sim, thanks for the follow-up. </p>
<p><em>“Wasn’t I supposed to take 71.3 minus 71 to get 0.3kg which is how much I gained per month?”</em></p>
<p>Technically, this is how much weight you gained in a 4-week period, yes. But weight captures not just muscle and fat mass changes, but also water, glycogen, and gut content.</p>
<p>The reason I said to look for a trend line is that any given week may be an anomaly that doesn’t fit the rest of the pattern, precisely because of these non-fat/muscle fluctuations.</p>
<p>If you ignore the first week, what do you see? Nothing, right? I recommend making an increase to get yourself on track.</p>
<p>My book, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/adjustments-manual/">The Diet Adjustments Manual</a>, has all of this kind of thing covered and may be of interest.</p>

</div>

    <p>Hi Andy your article has been really helpful and appreciate your information<br>

<p>Obviously my body weight is on the low side so I was wondering what approach you would recommend towards bulking and cutting duration- after I’ve reached the end of the 6 months would you recommend extending the bulk duration or starting a cut phase (wether that be a short mini cut or a traditional 8-12 week cut)</p>

  var optinMonsterSegment = 'generic'; // Default

  // Slug arrays
  var trainingSegmentSlugs = ['training']; // Add more training slugs as needed
  var nutritionSegmentSlugs = ['nutrition']; // Add more nutrition slugs as needed

  // Function to check if any slug exists in body class
  function segmentSlugExists(slugArray) {
    return slugArray.some(function(slug) {
      return bodyClassList.contains('category-' + slug);
    });
  }

  // Check for training or nutrition segments
  if (segmentSlugExists(trainingSegmentSlugs)) {
    optinMonsterSegment = 'training';
  } else if (segmentSlugExists(nutritionSegmentSlugs)) {
    optinMonsterSegment = 'nutrition';
  }

  console.log('OptinMonster Segment: ' + optinMonsterSegment);
  
  window.optinMonsterSegment = optinMonsterSegment;

  // Add segment-specific behavior for OptinMonster if necessary</script> <script>/(trident|msie)/i.test(navigator.userAgent)&&document.getElementById&&window.addEventListener&&window.addEventListener("hashchange",function(){var t,e=location.hash.substring(1);/^[A-z0-9_-]+$/.test(e)&&(t=document.getElementById(e))&&(/^(?:a|select|input|button|textarea)$/i.test(t.tagName)||(t.tabIndex=-1),t.focus())},!1);</script> <script id="astra-theme-js-js-extra">var astra = {"break_point":"1250","isRtl":"","is_scroll_to_id":"","is_scroll_to_top":"1","is_header_footer_builder_active":"","responsive_cart_click":"flyout","is_dark_palette":"","revealEffectEnable":"","edit_post_url":"https://rippedbody.com/wp-admin/post.php?post={{id}}&action=edit","ajax_url":"https://rippedbody.com/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php","infinite_count":"2","infinite_total":"0","pagination":"infinite","infinite_scroll_event":"scroll","no_more_post_message":"\u003Ca href=\"/coaching/\" id=\"archive-no-more-posts-button\"\u003EAPPLY FOR COACHING\u003C/a\u003E","grid_layout":"1","site_url":"https://rippedbody.com","blogArchiveTitleLayout":"","blogArchiveTitleOn":"","show_comments":"Show Comments","enableHistoryPushState":"1","masonryEnabled":"","blogMasonryBreakPoint":"0"};

metadata.description: This 10,000-word bulking guide will show you how to adjust your diet to gain muscle, without making the mistakes that cause people to stagnate or get fat.
metadata.og:description: This 10,000-word bulking guide, based on coaching clients online over the last decade, will show you how to adjust your diet to gain muscle, without making the mistakes that cause people to stagnate or get fat.
metadata.og:title: How to Bulk Without Getting Fat — The Definitive Guide To Bulking
